does: This paper presents Gate AI, a rigorous evaluation methodology for prompt-injection and jailbreak detectors in large language models. The core contribution is an evaluation harness that tests detectors across 16 public benchmarks containing 12,111 samples using 5-fold cross-validation, establishing a single global operating point (maximum F1 score subject to false positive rate ≤ 1%) applied uniformly across all datasets rather than per-dataset threshold tuning. The methodology includes a parallel StratifiedGroupKFold validation pass that detects data leakage through near-duplicate clustering using MinHash and locality-sensitive hashing at Jaccard similarity thresholds ≥ 0.8, providing diagnostic insights into detector generalization beyond standard metrics.
connects: This work directly addresses systematic flaws in existing prompt-injection and jailbreak detector evaluations that have proliferated as LLM security concerns have grown. It builds on established machine learning evaluation practices—stratified k-fold cross-validation, held-out testing protocols, and operating point selection—while adapting them specifically to the challenges of adversarial robustness assessment in language models. The methodology responds to prior work in prompt security and jailbreak detection by recognizing that undisclosed thresholds and per-dataset optimization create incomparable results across the literature. It integrates modern near-duplicate detection techniques from information retrieval to address a problem overlooked in earlier security benchmarking efforts.
enables: This standardized evaluation framework enables fair comparison of competing prompt-injection and jailbreak detection approaches, facilitating progress toward genuinely robust LLM security mechanisms. By establishing transparent operating points and accounting for data leakage through near-duplicate analysis, the methodology supports the development of detectors with verifiable out-of-distribution generalization. The diagnostic battery—including adversarial validation and feature importance analysis—creates pathways for understanding failure modes and informing detector improvements. Future work can build upon this foundation to develop more sophisticated detection methods with documented performance guarantees, support standardized security evaluation practices across the broader ML community, and guide deployment decisions with clearer operating-point tradeoffs between security and usability.
